title: MAKING INTRODUCTIONS
conversation: At the dinner party, Lisa introduces Lori and Mike to Jane and Kyle Chen. Lori and Jane realize they’ve met previously through work. Lisa: Let me make some introductions. Lori and Mike Garcia, this is Jane and Kyle Chen. Lori: Nice to meet you. Kyle: I’m sorry, I didn’t catch your names. Lori: I’m Lori and this is my husband Mike. Lisa: You guys have a lot in common, so I’m sure you’ll hit it off. Jane: Lori, you look familiar, but I can’t quite place you. Lori: Your name rings a bell. Do you work at Harco Insurance? Jane: I used to be in sales there. I left about a year ago. Lori: I used to work at Comtek International. You sold us our insurance plan. Jane: Yes, that’s right! It’s a small world! Lori: It sure is. I’m glad we’ve crossed paths again. Jane: Me too. What have you been up to since you left Comtek? Lori: It’s a long story. Let’s grab some drinks and I’ll fill you in.

idioms.meaning_en: To present people to each other by name for the first time
idioms.example: Before we start the meeting, let me make some introductions.

idioms.meaning_en: To fail to hear or understand what someone's names were when they were spoken
idioms.example: I'm sorry, the room was so loud that I didn't catch your names.
idioms.context: I’m sorry, I didn’t catch your names.

idioms.meaning_en: To share many of the same interests, attitudes, or characteristics
idioms.example: My best friend and I have a lot in common, especially our love for music.
idioms.context: You guys have a lot in common, so I’m sure you’ll hit it off.

idioms.meaning_en: To get along very well with someone from the first time you meet them
idioms.meaning_vi: tâm đầu ý hợp / nhanh chóng trở nên thân thiết
idioms.example: I introduced my two cousins, and they really hit it off.
idioms.context: You guys have a lot in common, so I’m sure you’ll hit it off.

idioms.meaning_en: To seem like someone or something you have seen or known before
idioms.example: You look familiar; did we go to the same high school?
idioms.context: Lori, you look familiar, but I can’t quite place you.

idioms.meaning_en: To remember someone's face but be unable to recall exactly who they are or where you met them
idioms.meaning_vi: không thể nhớ ra chính xác là ai/gặp ở đâu
idioms.example: I recognize your voice, but I can't quite place you.
idioms.context: Lori, you look familiar, but I can’t quite place you.

idioms.meaning_en: To sound vaguely familiar, or remind you of something you've heard before
idioms.meaning_vi: nghe quen quen / gợi nhớ đến điều gì
idioms.example: The name of that restaurant rings a bell, but I don't think I've eaten there.

idioms.meaning_en: Working in the department responsible for selling products or services
idioms.meaning_vi: làm trong bộ phận bán hàng/kinh doanh
idioms.example: He has been in sales for over ten years.
idioms.context: I used to be in sales there.

idioms.meaning_en: An expression used when meeting someone unexpectedly or discovering a mutual connection
idioms.meaning_vi: trái đất thật tròn / thế giới thật nhỏ bé
idioms.example: I saw my old neighbor in Paris; it's a small world!
idioms.context: Yes, that’s right! It’s a small world!

idioms.meaning_en: To meet someone, usually by chance or accident
idioms.example: We first crossed paths at a technology conference last year.
idioms.context: I’m glad we’ve crossed paths again.

idioms.idiom: What have you been up to
idioms.meaning_en: A common way to ask what someone has been doing lately
idioms.meaning_vi: Dạo này bạn làm gì rồi?
idioms.example: It's been a long time! What have you been up to?
idioms.context: What have you been up to since you left Comtek?

idioms.meaning_en: Used to indicate that something is complicated and would take a long time to explain fully
idioms.meaning_vi: đó là một câu chuyện dài (khó giải thích nhanh)
idioms.example: Why did I quit my job? It's a long story.

idioms.meaning_en: To go out to have something to drink, usually socially
idioms.meaning_vi: đi uống nước / đi làm vài ly
idioms.example: Let's grab some drinks after work on Friday.
idioms.context: Let’s grab some drinks and I’ll fill you in.

idioms.meaning_en: To give someone the information or details that they have missed
idioms.meaning_vi: cung cấp chi tiết/kể cho ai đó nghe thông tin
idioms.example: I missed the meeting, so could you fill me in on what happened?
idioms.context: Let’s grab some drinks and I’ll fill you in.
